skill.name: agent-ui
skill.description: Build React and Next.js agent interfaces with a drop-in component, streaming, approvals, widgets, and browser-side tools.
skill.summary: Add a full agent UI component to React or Next.js applications.
skill.author: inference-sh-9

security_audit.summary: Static command-execution and weak-cryptography alerts are mostly false positives caused by Markdown code fences and ordinary text. The skill still carries medium supply-chain and secret-handling risk because it instructs users to fetch remote component code, install an SDK package, and configure an API key backed proxy.

medium_findings.title: Remote Component and Package Installation
medium_findings.description: The Quick Start asks users to run npx shadcn with a remote registry URL and install @inferencesh/sdk. This is a legitimate setup path, but it creates supply-chain risk because remote code and package install behavior must be trusted before execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are directly documented and fetch code from an external registry or package source. I found no evidence that the package is malicious, so the concern is supply-chain exposure rather than confirmed abuse.

medium_findings.title: API Key Proxy Configuration
medium_findings.description: The setup creates a Next.js proxy route and stores INFERENCE_API_KEY in .env.local. This is expected for the service, but it handles credentials and forwards application traffic through a generated route.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The API key and proxy route are explicit in the instructions. I found no evidence of exfiltration, but incorrect route exposure or key handling would affect user secrets.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged many Markdown code fences and inline prop names as Ruby or shell backtick execution. SKILL.md is documentation, and these backticks do not execute by themselves.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged syntax appears inside Markdown fences or inline documentation. There is no Ruby file, shell script, eval path, or automatic execution mechanism in the scanned file.

low_findings.title: External URLs Are Documentation and Asset References
low_findings.description: Several hardcoded URLs point to the product site, an image asset, documentation, and blog pages. These links are external network references, but they are visible documentation references rather than hidden outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are visible Markdown links or image references. I found no evidence that the skill silently sends data to these URLs.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Not Confirmed
low_findings.description: Static weak-cryptography alerts at the listed lines do not correspond to cryptographic code. The lines contain metadata, descriptive prose, a model reference, or a Markdown table heading.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: I inspected each reported line and found no hash, cipher, signature, random-number, or certificate implementation. These are false positives from text scanning.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Run Remote Installer Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents npx commands that retrieve a component registry entry and related skills. These commands should be reviewed before execution in trusted projects.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and rely on external package or registry content. The file provides no checksum, version pin, or sandbox guidance.

dangerous_patterns.title: Credential-Backed Proxy Route
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented proxy route uses an API key from .env.local and exposes HTTP methods through an SDK route helper. Review authentication and origin controls before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The setup clearly depends on an API key and route helper. The documentation does not show access-control details, so deployment review is required.

content.user_title: Build Agent UIs for React and Next.js
content.value_statement: Teams need agent interfaces that handle streaming, approvals, tools, and UI widgets without repeated custom work. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through adding the agent-ui component to a React or Next.js project.
actual_capabilities: Installs an agent component from the ui.inference.sh shadcn registry.
Adds the @inferencesh/sdk dependency for a Next.js proxy route.
Shows how to configure an Inference API key in .env.local.
Demonstrates an Agent component with streaming and approval features.
Shows client-side scoped tools for form scanning and field filling.
Links to related agent, widget, and tool lifecycle documentation.
limitations: Requires trust in external registry content and npm package sources.
Assumes a React or Next.js project that can use shadcn-style components.
Requires an INFERENCE_API_KEY for the documented proxy route.
Does not include production security controls for the proxy route.

use_cases.title: Add an AI assistant to a SaaS app
use_cases.description: Install the component and configure a proxy route for a product copilot inside a Next.js application.

use_cases.title: Prototype an agent workflow
use_cases.description: Create an interface that can stream responses, request approvals, and display generated widgets.

use_cases.title: Connect browser tools to forms
use_cases.description: Use scoped client tools so an agent can inspect a form and fill fields with user approval.

prompt_templates.title: Install the agent component
prompt_templates.prompt: Add the agent-ui component to my Next.js app. Show each command before running it and explain any external dependency.

prompt_templates.title: Create the proxy route
prompt_templates.prompt: Set up the Inference proxy route for my app. Keep the API key in environment variables and do not expose it to client code.

prompt_templates.title: Embed the Agent component
prompt_templates.prompt: Add the Agent component to this page and configure it for streaming responses, approvals, and a clear assistant name.

prompt_templates.title: Add scoped client tools
prompt_templates.prompt: Wire scoped browser tools into the Agent component so it can scan a form and fill fields after user approval.

output_examples.input: Install agent-ui in a new Next.js project.
output: A short list of required commands.
A note that the component is fetched from ui.inference.sh.
A reminder to review external code before running it.

output_examples.input: Add a secure proxy route for the agent component.
output: A route setup plan for the Next.js app.
Security checks for server-only API key handling.

output_examples.input: Enable form filling with client-side tools.
output: A description of scoped tool behavior.
Approval and testing steps for browser actions.

best_practices: Review registry and package changes before running installation commands.
Store INFERENCE_API_KEY only in server-side environment files.
Add authentication, rate limits, and origin checks to production proxy routes.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys into client components or public repositories.
Do not run remote installer commands without reviewing the source.
Do not enable browser tools to change forms without user approval.

faq.question: What does this skill help build?
faq.answer: It helps add an agent interface to React or Next.js apps with streaming, approvals, widgets, and tool support.

faq.question: Does it require an external service?
faq.answer: Yes. The documented setup uses ui.inference.sh resources and an INFERENCE_API_KEY for the proxy route.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: Yes. The marketplace metadata marks the skill as compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Is the skill automatically executing commands?
faq.answer: No. The file documents commands, but an assistant or user may run them during setup.

faq.question: What should I review before publishing?
faq.answer: Review the remote registry component, npm dependency, API key handling, and proxy route access controls.

faq.question: Does it include production hardening?
faq.answer: No. The documentation shows setup steps, but production authentication and abuse protection need separate implementation.
